perturbation.original_text: Each of the Parties hereby agrees that the prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to full recovery of its attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Parties hereby agrees that the prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to full recovery of its attorneys’ fees. This provision only applies if both parties agree in writing after the dispute arises.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the attorney's fees clause into a section about miscellaneous terms makes it harder to find. Furthermore, adding the clause 'This provision only applies if both parties agree in writing after the dispute arises' effectively nullifies the original clause. Under District of Columbia law, contracts should be enforced as written unless there is a clear reason not to, and this change adds an ambiguity that could prevent enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts D.C. Official Code § 28:1-304, which states that contracts should be interpreted to give effect to the parties' intentions. By adding a condition that requires a separate written agreement after a dispute arises, the original intent of the attorney's fees clause is undermined, making it more difficult to enforce the original agreement and deviating from the expected performance under the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 28:1â304. Obligation of good faith. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 28:1â304. Obligation of good faith. Every contract or duty within this subtitle imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. ( Dec. 30, 1963, 77 Stat. 636, Pub. L. 88-243, Â§ 1 ; Apr. 27, 2013, D.C. Law 19-299, Â§ 2, 60 DCR 2634 .) Prior Codifications 2001 Ed., Â§ 28:1-203. Uniform Commercial Code Comment Source: Former Section 1-203. Changes from former law: Except for changing the form of reference to the Uniform Commercial Code, this section is identical to former Section 1-203. 1. This section sets forth a basic principle running throughout the Uniform Commercial Code. The principle is that in commercial transactions good faith is required in the performance and enforcement of all agreements or duties. While this duty is explicitly stated in some provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code, the applicability of the duty is broader than merely these situations and applies generally, as stated in this section, to the performance or enforcement of every contract or duty within this Act. It is further implemented by Section 1-303 on course of dealing, course of performance, and usage of trade. This section does not support an independent cause of action for failure to perform or enforce in good faith. Rather, this section means that a failure to perform or enforce, in good faith, a specific duty or obligation under the contract, constitutes a breach of that contract or makes unavailable, under the particular circumstances, a remedial right or power. This distinction makes it clear that the doctrine of good faith merely directs a court towards interpreting contracts within the commercial context in which they are created, performed, and enforced, and does not create a separate duty of fairness and reasonableness which can be independently breached. 2. âPerformance and enforcementâ of contracts and duties within the Uniform Commercial
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a condition that effectively nullifies the attorney's fees clause unless both parties agree in writing after a dispute arises. This undermines the original intent of the clause and contradicts the principle that contracts should be interpreted to give effect to the parties' intentions. Snippet 1 highlights that contracts should be performed and enforced in good faith, which this modification arguably violates by creating an easily invoked loophole to avoid paying attorney's fees. The added condition deviates from the expected performance under the contract, creating a structural flaw that weakens enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the District of Columbia, without regard to principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the District of Columbia, without regard to principles of conflicts of laws. However, for any matters relating to intellectual property, the laws of the state where the intellectual property was first created shall govern.
perturbation.explanation: The added clause introduces a conflict of laws issue by stating that intellectual property matters are governed by the law of the state where the IP was created. This is placed in the general governing law section, which initially specifies that D.C. law governs the entire agreement. This could create confusion and ambiguity as to which jurisdiction's law applies, especially if the intellectual property was created outside of D.C. This contradicts the principle of clear governing law in contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts D.C. Official Code § 28:1-105, which allows parties to agree on the governing law. The added language creates ambiguity and undermines the initial agreement to have D.C. law govern all aspects of the contract by introducing another potential governing law for intellectual property issues. It makes the application of D.C. law uncertain, thus contravening the purpose of a clear choice-of-law provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 28:1â105. Severability. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 28:1â105. Severability. If any provision or clause of this subtitle or its application to any person or circumstance is held invalid, the invalidity does not affect other provisions or applications of this subtitle which can be given effect without the invalid provision or application, and to this end the provisions of this subtitle are severable. ( Dec. 30, 1963, 77 Stat. 632, Pub. L. 88-243, Â§ 1 ; Apr. 27, 2013, D.C. Law 19-299, Â§ 2, 60 DCR 2634 .) Prior Codifications 2001 Ed., Â§ 28:1-108. 1981 Ed., Â§â28:1-108. 1973 Ed., Â§â28:1-108. Uniform Commercial Code Comment Source: Former Section 1-108. Changes from former law: Except for changing the form of reference to the Uniform Commercial Code, this section is identical to former Section 1-108. 1. This is the model severability section recommended by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws for inclusion in all acts of extensive scope.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a structural flaw that creates a legal contradiction. The original text clearly states that the laws of the District of Columbia govern the agreement. The changed text introduces an exception for intellectual property, stating that the laws of the state where the IP was created will govern. This creates ambiguity and potential conflicts regarding which jurisdiction's law applies, especially if the intellectual property was created outside of D.C. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this issue, stating that it contradicts the principle of clear governing law in contracts. The Law Explanation further supports this by stating that the modification undermines the initial agreement and makes the application of D.C. law uncertain. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses severability, it doesn't address the core issue of conflicting governing laws introduced by the modification. The introduction of a second, potentially conflicting, governing law creates a structural flaw and legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In addition to all other legal rights and remedies the disclosing Party may have upon a breach of this Agreement by the receiving Party, the disclosing Party shall be entitled to specific performance or injunctive relief respecting the covenants contained herein, and the receiving Party expressly waives the defense that a remedy in damages will be adequate.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition to all other legal rights and remedies the disclosing Party may have upon a breach of this Agreement by the receiving Party, the disclosing Party shall be entitled to specific performance or injunctive relief respecting the covenants contained herein, and the receiving Party expressly waives the defense that a remedy in damages will be adequate. However, the availability of specific performance or injunctive relief will be determined by the receiving party's legal counsel at the time of breach.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'However, the availability of specific performance or injunctive relief will be determined by the receiving party's legal counsel at the time of breach' effectively nullifies the disclosing party's right to specific performance or injunctive relief. It gives the receiving party the power to unilaterally decide whether such remedies are available, contradicting the original intent and creating an unenforceable provision.
perturbation.law_explanation: The inserted clause potentially violates D.C. Official Code § 28:2-302, which addresses unconscionable contracts or clauses. By giving one party (the receiving party) the unilateral power to determine the availability of equitable remedies like specific performance or injunctive relief, the amended clause could be considered unconscionable as it creates a significant imbalance of power and unfairly prejudices the disclosing party. The clause undermines the fundamental principle that contract terms should be mutually agreed upon and fair to both parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 28:2â302. Unconscionable contract or clause. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 28:2â302. Unconscionable contract or clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. ( Dec. 30, 1963, 77 Stat. 645, Pub. L. 88-243, Â§ 1 .) Prior Codifications 1981 Ed., Â§â28:2-302. 1973 Ed., Â§â28:2-302. Uniform Commercial Code Comment Prior Uniform Statutory Provision: None. Purposes: 1. This section is intended to make it possible for the courts to police explicitly against the contracts or clauses which they find to be unconscionable. In the past such policing has been accomplished by adverse construction of language, by manipulation of the rules of offer and acceptance or by determinations that the clause is contrary to public policy or to the dominant purpose of the contract. This section is intended to allow the court to pass directly on the unconscionability of the contract or particular clause therein and to make a conclusion of law as to its unconscionability. The basic test is whether, in the light of the general commercial background and the commercial needs of the particular trade or case, the clauses involved are so one-sided as to be unconscionable under the circumstances existing at the time of the making of the contract. Subsection (2) makes it clear that it is proper for the court to hear evidence upon these questions. The principle i
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause granting the receiving party's legal counsel the power to determine the availability of specific performance or injunctive relief, which significantly alters the disclosing party's rights and creates an imbalance of power. This contradicts the principle that contract terms should be mutually agreed upon and fair to both parties. D.C. Official Code § 28:2-302, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, addresses unconscionable contracts or clauses, allowing courts to refuse to enforce contracts or clauses found to be unconscionable. The modified clause could be deemed unconscionable because it is one-sided, potentially undermining the disclosing party's equitable remedies.

perturbation.original_text: Once the purpose of the disclosure is achieved, the HNBA will instruct the Board Member to destroy all Confidential Information supplied to the Board Member by the HNBA. The HNBA will keep a copy in its files for a period of two (2) years after the completion of each Candidate’s specific request.
perturbation.changed_text: Once the purpose of the disclosure is achieved, the HNBA will instruct the Board Member to destroy all Confidential Information supplied to the Board Member by the HNBA. The HNBA will keep a copy in its files for a period of two (2) years after the completion of each Candidate’s specific request. However, if the Board Member believes that retaining the information is in their best personal interest, they are not obligated to destroy it.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the clause 'However, if the Board Member believes that retaining the information is in their best personal interest, they are not obligated to destroy it' directly contradicts the initial instruction to destroy all confidential information after the purpose is achieved. This undermines the confidentiality agreement, as it gives the Board Member the unilateral right to retain confidential information indefinitely, irrespective of the agreement's purpose.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified clause contradicts D.C. Official Code § 28:1-203, which implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in the performance and enforcement of contracts. By allowing the Board Member to unilaterally decide to retain confidential information based on their 'best personal interest,' the modification undermines the reasonable expectations of the HNBA and deviates from the principle of fair dealing. This gives the Board Member the right to avoid the obligations which were agreed upon, thus breaching the covenant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 28:1â203. Lease distinguished from security interest. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 28:1â203. Lease distinguished from security interest. (a) Whether a transaction in the form of a lease creates a lease or security interest is determined by the facts of each case. (b) A transaction in the form of a lease creates a security interest if the consideration that the lessee is to pay the lessor for the right to possession and use of the goods is an obligation for the term of the lease and is not subject to termination by the lessee, and: (1) The original term of the lease is equal to or greater than the remaining economic life of the goods; (2) The lessee is bound to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods or is bound to become the owner of the goods; (3) The lessee has an option to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement; or (4) The lessee has an option to become the owner of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement. (c) A transaction in the form of a lease does not create a security interest merely because: (1) The present value of the consideration the lessee is obligated to pay the lessor for the right to possession and use of the goods is substantially equal to or is greater than the fair market value of the goods at the time the lease is entered into; (2) The lessee assumes risk of loss of the goods; (3) The lessee agrees to pay, with respect to the goods, taxes, insurance, filing, recording, or registration fees, or service or maintenance costs; (4) The lessee has an option to renew the lease or to become the owner of the goods; (5) The lessee has an option to renew the lease for a fixed rent that is equal to or greater than the reasonably predictable fair market rent for the use of the goods
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction because it allows the Board Member to retain confidential information based on their personal interest, which undermines the initial instruction to destroy such information after the purpose of disclosure is achieved. This contradicts the principle of fair dealing implied in contracts. While the provided snippet discusses lease agreements and security interests, the Law Explanation references D.C. Official Code § 28:1-203 which implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in contract performance. The modified clause allows the Board Member to potentially act in bad faith by prioritizing personal interest over the agreed-upon confidentiality obligations, thus creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Board Member agrees that the Confidential Information he or she receives shall be used solely for the benefit of the HNBA Board Work, and that all rights to the proprietary and novel features contained in the Confidential Information are reserved by the disclosing Party. The Board Member will not use or disclose Confidential Information to any third party for any purpose other than the performance of his or her duties as a Board Member of the HNBA unless and until the HNBA expressly authorizes the disclosure in writing. Board Member specifically agrees not to use any personally identifiable information provided by or on behalf of the HNBA, its contractors, affiliates, vendors, sponsors or employees, for any direct marketing and not to transfer such information to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Board Member agrees that the Confidential Information he or she receives shall be used solely for the benefit of the HNBA Board Work, and that all rights to the proprietary and novel features contained in the Confidential Information are reserved by the disclosing Party. The Board Member will not use or disclose Confidential Information to any third party for any purpose other than the performance of his or her duties as a Board Member of the HNBA unless and until the HNBA expressly authorizes the disclosure in writing. Board Member specifically agrees not to use any personally identifiable information provided by or on behalf of the HNBA, its contractors, affiliates, vendors, sponsors or employees, for any direct marketing and not to transfer such information to any third party. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Board Member is permitted to use aggregated, anonymized data derived from the Confidential Information for personal research or publication, provided no individual or entity is directly identifiable.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the clause 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Board Member is permitted to use aggregated, anonymized data derived from the Confidential Information for personal research or publication, provided no individual or entity is directly identifiable' introduces an ambiguity that allows the Board Member to potentially circumvent the confidentiality restrictions. Even if the data is anonymized, there is still a risk that it could be re-identified or that the use of such data could indirectly harm the HNBA or its members, especially when derived from 'personally identifiable information.'
perturbation.law_explanation: The added clause contradicts the spirit, if not the strict letter, of D.C. Official Code § 28-3851 regarding consumer personal information security. While the added text specifies the data must be anonymized, the broad exception allowing usage for personal research opens the door for data breaches and inadvertent disclosures. This is because the anonymization process itself can be flawed, and depending on the nature of the data set, it may be possible to re-identify the data, violating the spirit of information security and the expectation of privacy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 28â3851. Definitions. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 28â3851. Definitions. For purposes of this subchapter, the term: (1) (A) "Breach of the security of the system" means unauthorized acquisition of computerized or other electronic data or any equipment or device storing such data that compromises the security, confidentiality, or integrity of personal information maintained by the person or entity who conducts business in the District of Columbia. (B) The term "breach of the security of the system" does not include: (i) A good-faith acquisition of personal information by an employee or agency of the person or entity for the purposes of the person or entity if the personal information is not used improperly or subject to further unauthorized disclosure; (ii) Acquisition of data that has been rendered secure, including through encryption or redaction of such data, so as to be unusable by an unauthorized third party unless any information obtained has the potential to compromise the effectiveness of the security protection preventing unauthorized access; or (iii) Acquisition of personal information of an individual that the person or entity reasonably determines, after a reasonable investigation and consultation with the Office of the Attorney General for the District of Columbia and federal law enforcement agencies, will likely not result in harm to the individual. (1A) "Genetic information" has the meaning ascribed to it under the federal Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 ("HIPAA"), approved August 21, 1996 (Pub. Law 104-191; 110 Stat. 1936), as specified in 45 C.F.R. Â§ 106.103. (1B) "Medical Information" means any information about a consumer's dental, medical, or mental health treatment or diagnosis by a health-care professional. (2) âNotifyâ or ânotificationâ means providing information through any of the following methods: (A) Written notice; (B) Electronic notice, if the customer has con
perturbation.justification: The added clause allows the Board Member to use 'aggregated, anonymized data' for personal research, derived from Confidential Information. This contradicts the security and confidentiality requirements of personal information, as highlighted by D.C. Official Code § 28-3851. The code defines a 'breach of the security of the system' as unauthorized acquisition of data that compromises the security, confidentiality, or integrity of personal information. While the clause stipulates anonymization, the risk of re-identification or inadvertent disclosure still exists, potentially leading to a breach. The snippet also highlights that 'Acquisition of data that has been rendered secure...so as to be unusable by an unauthorized third party' is not a breach unless 'any information obtained has the potential to compromise the effectiveness of the security protection preventing unauthorized access'. The addition of this clause undermines the overall confidentiality and data protection intended by the original contract.
